storytellers.bio: Iris is a dedicated caseworker devoted to empowering women navigating the challenges of substance abuse and everyday struggles. With a compassionate, supportive approach, Iris fosters resilience and community bonds, making a profound impact in her role. Part of the Ferdys team, she connects deeply with those she helps, becoming a trusted figure within the community. Her work extends beyond individual support to nurturing a welcoming environment where women can share stories and craft connections, reinforcing a sense of family and belonging.
Co-presented "Proud Bwgcolman Youth" at the 2023 SNAICC Conference, sharing a hopeful narrative about Palm Island young people.

Freddy Wai, a resilient and hopeful soul, navigates life in Melbourne after finding asylum from Papua Newa. His journey as a refugee has been both challenging and rewarding, offering profound lessons amidst the adversities of homelessness. Despite losing trust in institutions, Freddy's determination shines as he strives for dignity and essential rights, underscoring the universal need for shelter and community. His experiences in both Fiji and Australia highlight the complexities of adapting to new environments, where punctuality and survival intertwine in unexpected ways. Freddy's story is a testament to perseverance, showcasing his unwavering commitment to rebuilding his life.

Rhonda Phillips has been a PICC Board Director since the organisation's founding in 2007. With 40+ years of experience across community, public, and academic sectors, she brings deep expertise in Indigenous governance and service integration. Rhonda has chaired both the Governance Committee and the Finance & Risk Committee.

Luella Bligh is the Chairperson of the PICC Board, pivotal in the 2021 transition to full community control. She has noted the transition succeeded because PICC was already operating with a majority local board and up to 90% local workforce.

Matthew Lindsay is a PICC Board Director bringing financial governance expertise. He holds the designation of Fellow of CPA Australia (FCPA) and is a Graduate of the Australian Institute of Company Directors.
Harriet Hulthen is a PICC Community Director, born and raised on Palm Island. She brings experience from the Townsville Aboriginal and Islander Legal Service to her governance role.

Marilyn Laner, a devoted and vibrant pillar of the Kora community for over 40 years, embodies connection and compassion every Wednesday morning. A true "people collector," Marilyn's rich experiences span from her early days in the Boyan Valley to her extensive volunteer work with children and the elderly. Despite personal hardships, including the loss of two brothers and overcoming societal challenges alongside her Italian husband, Marilyn has remained steadfast in her belief in the power of human connection. Her warmth and advocacy for the less fortunate, whether through lending an ear or assembling warm meals, illuminate her enduring legacy of giving. Marilyn's life is a testament to the strength found in shared stories and the vibrant community she continues to nurture.

Generic team storyteller for the Women's Shelter staff conversation content. Synthetic record - staff describes domestic and family violence service work but no specific name surfaces in the opening. Replace with a real storyteller when PICC verifies who the speaker(s) were.

Ethel and Iris Ferdies are pillars of resilience and familial devotion, deeply rooted in their dynamic family history. Growing up in a family of 17, Ethel and Iris witnessed firsthand the struggles and triumphs of their mother, a member of the Stolen Generations, who raised them with fortitude and love. Despite the hardships of their upbringing—defined by chores, loss, and the need to support each other—Ethel speaks warmly of the lessons learned from their mother, a woman of the Durable tribe with exceptional culinary skills. Both sisters, marked by the legacy of displacement yet anchored by community and tradition, embody strength and unity through their lived experiences. Their narratives are a testament to overcoming adversity and maintaining cultural heritage.

Raymond W. Palmer Snr. is a Bwgcolman man and lifelong Palm Island resident who was appointed to the PICC Board in 2023. He works as a Teacher's Aide at Bwgcolman Community School, bringing grassroots community perspective to PICC governance.

Roy Prior is a devoted community advocate and cultural custodian, deeply rooted in the serene landscapes of Palmer. Returning to his island home to care for his ailing grandmother, Roy has since become an integral part of the community's fabric, cherishing its natural beauty and laid-back spirit. His extensive family ties stretch across the region, from Townsville to Cairns, fostering connections that reflect his commitment to kinship and community. Since joining the local initiative, Pick, in 2011, Roy has been pivotal in grassroots efforts, enhancing community engagement and support. His enduring presence and dedication have left a lasting impact, strengthening bonds and nurturing the island's vibrant cultural tapestry.

Jess Smit is an inspiring educator and project officer deeply rooted in Townsville, where she has spent the last 18 years transforming young lives. With a strong foundation in teaching, Jess has passionately devoted herself to the ATSIMS program, working with First Nations high school students to explore marine science and foster new experiences on the reef. Her journey comes full circle on Palm Island, where she began her career, now collaborating with the Orpheus Island Station Crew, the Minga Minga Ranger Group, and the Mumba Traditional Owners. Jess is driven by a profound sense of purpose in empowering others, especially women in marine environments, celebrating shared stories and traditions while building capacities within her community.

Paige Tanner Hill is a passionate marine advocate from Palm Island, dedicated to preserving and connecting with their rich cultural heritage. At just 18, Paige is making waves as a junior ranger, immersing themselves in diving ventures and historical explorations to understand the past and rejuvenate community ties. A recent graduate of Wuggerman Community School, they are committed to engaging local youth in environmental stewardship through the ranger program, blending modern initiatives with ancestral wisdom. With each dive at sites like the Two Sisters reef, Paige uncovers a breathtaking underwater world, eager to share its stories and their unyielding beauty.

Natalie Friday is a dedicated cultural and environmental advocate rooted deeply in her Eastern Googiallanji and Okla heritage. Born in Townsville and raised on the resilient lands of Palm Island, Natalie carries the legacy of her ancestors who were forcibly relocated under the Aboriginal Protection Act. Her life's journey, steeped in the traditions of Cape York Peninsula and enriched by her role as a Bulgaman woman, fuels her commitment to cultural preservation and natural resource management. Through her impactful work with the Working on Country Ranger Program and her efforts with NQ Dry Tropics and the North Queensland Land Council, Natalie intricately weaves ancestral wisdom into modern environmental stewardship, empowering her community and honoring her storied lineage.

Henry Doyle is a dedicated youth advocate and proud Aboriginal resident of Palm Island, whose passion for his community is evident in his work with disengaged youth. Living on the island since birth, Henry combines his love for sports, particularly footy, with his commitment to keeping young people active and engaged. Through his role in youth services, he designs programs that offer youth meaningful opportunities, from securing driver's and boat licenses to participating in community service. His efforts extend to nearby Townsville, where he supports the next generation by cultivating a sense of belonging and resilience, ensuring they steer clear of mischief. Henry's vibrant presence and unwavering dedication make a profound impact, offering hope and guidance to those who need it most.

Uncle George Leon, a distinguished Kalkadoon elder, embodies resilience and community spirit in Mount Isa, where his roots run deep since his family's arrival in 1947. Raised in a vibrant household that fostered camaraderie among thousands, including local rugby league players, Uncle George learned the value of community early on. Known for his 'tough love' approach, he has dedicated his life to steering youth towards education, emphasizing the importance of engaging with children in school to curb crime and nurture potential. His unique role as a PLO was instrumental in connecting with young people and reinforcing community trust, making Uncle George a beloved and respected figure whose impact spans generations.

Jacqui is a dedicated public health advocate and compassionate volunteer with Orange Sky, a group committed to enhancing dignity and fostering genuine connections among those in need. Originally joining the cause in 2018, Jacqui brings a wealth of experience from her public health background. Her passion for service is deeply personal, driven by her familiarity with diverse social backgrounds and the impact of witnessing a family member facing difficult times. Known for her empathetic nature, Jacqui finds joy in continuing meaningful conversations with the friends she’s made, reinforcing the community’s spirit through simple, yet profound acts of kindness like providing laundry services.

Generic team storyteller for the Palm Island Community Hub walk-around interview content. Synthetic record - no specific individual identified. Replace with a real storyteller when PICC verifies who the speaker(s) were.

Generic team storyteller for the Women's Healing Service staff conversation content. Synthetic record - transcript references John as MDI lead but the actual speaker was not clearly self-identified. Replace with a real storyteller when PICC verifies who the speaker(s) were.

Allan Palm Island is a Traditional Owner Director on the PICC Board, representing the Manbarra people (Wulgurukaba). He learned cultural traditions from his father starting at age 8 and studied the stories of the Nanggarra Wanggarra people. Allan holds a Master of Fine Arts from RMIT University Melbourne (1999). His art practice depicts land and sea life, embodying the spiritual connection to ancestors and Country.
Cassie Lang is a PICC Board Director bringing 14 years of legal expertise in Native Title and Indigenous Cultural Heritage. She is the Founder and Principal Solicitor of Parallax Legal.

Rachel Atkinson is the inaugural Chief Executive Officer of Palm Island Community Company, serving since 2007. A Yorta Yorta woman with a family lineage of activists — her great uncle William Cooper and cousin Sir Douglas Nicholls (first Indigenous Governor of South Australia) — Rachel holds a Bachelor of Social Work and previously led the Townsville Aboriginal and Islander Health Service (1996-2006). She transformed PICC from a single employee to 210+ staff, with 95% local employment generating $5.8M in annual wages. Rachel led the 14-year campaign that achieved full community control on September 30, 2021. She co-chairs the Queensland First Children and Families Board and Family Matters Queensland.
Staff from Palm Island Early Learning Centre.

Manager of PICC Children and Youth Services. Presented "Proud Bwgcolman Youth" at the 2023 SNAICC Conference, proposing an alternative approach to tackling the complex issues driving the over-representation of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in the youth justice system.

Agnes Watton is a Palm Island elder who weathered the storm alone, her story a testament to the resilience and independence of the island's older generation. Her narrative captures the reality of life on Palm Island — the challenges of isolation, infrastructure, and the strength required to endure.

Alice, an unwavering community pillar, navigates the intricate world of childcare with resilience and compassion. A cornerstone during challenging times, she transforms setbacks into opportunities for growth, ensuring continuity and care for her community. Her unwavering dedication, even as critical infrastructure faced disruptions, showcases her commitment to maintaining stability and nurturing an environment where everyone feels supported. Whether mopping floors or orchestrating routines amidst chaos, Alice’s leadership shines, reflecting her steadfast commitment to the well-being of the children and families she serves.

Winifred Obah is a cultural advisor and secretary for the Elders Advisory on Palm Island. A WA woman whose lineage intertwines Palm Island and Cairns through her great-grandfather Ganji and grandfather Allison Oba, Winnie organises cultural reconnection journeys — including the two-year effort to return to Mission Beach, funded by homemade cookies, tarts, and cakes.

Elder Gurtrude Grace Richardson is a revered member of the Palm Island community whose journey from Georgetown to decades on Palm Island has been marked by challenges and triumphs. Her story of resilience and belonging reflects the strength of Aboriginal women who build community through hardship.

Irene Nleallajar is a passionate cultural custodian from Palm Island, deeply rooted in the rich heritage of the Boman people. Born in Townsville in the 1960s, Irene has witnessed remarkable changes across decades, drawing strength from her family's legacy of resilience and connection to the land. Her father's legacy as a fisherman and bush walker, as well as being the eldest of fifteen, informs her profound connection to nature and community. Irene embodies the spirit of her ancestors, sharing their stories and traditions with the next generation while celebrating the vibrant history and beauty of Palm Island.

Men's Group is a transformative collective born from the shared experiences of overcoming addiction and seeking genuine purpose beyond substances. Originating from personal struggles with alcohol, they inspire others to define their own daily motivations and break free from dependency. Unlike the mindless routines dictated by addiction, they emphasize intentional living and self-determined goals. Through candid dialogue, Men's Group empowers individuals to reevaluate their priorities, fostering a profound sense of satisfaction and accomplishment. Their unique contribution lies in guiding others to craft authentic purposes and enrich their everyday lives with intentionality and resilience.

Uncle Frank Daniel Landers: A resilient and reflective soul, Uncle Frank Daniel Landers hails from Ingham and carries the weight of history with an unyielding spirit. Born in 1945, his journey began when his family relocated and faced the trials of dormitory life, shaping his formative years with discipline and community reliance. Despite the challenges of being governed by strict rules, Uncle Frank found strength in his roots and eventually embraced life beyond the confines of the boys' homes. His unique narrative, spanning from separation during his mother's illness to mingling with newfound freedom in the village, is a testament to his endurance and adaptability. Through his vivid recollections, Uncle Frank shares a profound legacy of perseverance and connection to heritage.

Aunty Iris May Whitey is a sister from the Palm Island community who, alongside Ethel, carries the stories and struggles of their family with profound dignity. Raised in a family of seventeen, their mother was part of the Stolen Generations. Despite the deep scars of displacement, the family bond held strong — built on hard work, resilience, and the lessons passed down through generations.

Clay Alfred is a Palm Island elder whose voice resonates with wisdom and a vision for empowering community youth through meaningful engagement. He advocates for genuine dialogue over formal letters, dreaming of reconnecting young people with culture, Country, and purpose.

Elder Del Louise Pryor weaves a narrative of connection, loss, and resilience rooted in Palm Island's history. Her grandfather Peter Brear was caught up in the 1930s incident involving Robert Curry on the island. Her mother, a member of the Stolen Generation, shaped Del's deep commitment to healing and community.

Elder Ethel Taylor Robinson, 78, is a pillar of wisdom within the Palm Island community. A descendant of the IRI Manga Island through her father, a South AKA man born in Halifax, Ethel carries the stories of forced removals and the Stolen Generations — her mother was taken as a young girl. Born into a family of seventeen siblings, Ethel's narrative is one of resilience, cultural heritage, and an unbreakable bond to Country.

Elder Marjorie Burns carries memories woven with threads of resilience and cultural heritage. She recalls her mother's regret at not learning her language: "She said, I should have listened to my mother at that time." Her grandfather Alf Palmer's legacy connects her deeply to Palm Island's history and the importance of passing knowledge to the next generation.

Elsa Watson is a Cultural Advisor from Palm Island whose family's history is one of resilience and connection. Her mother Doreen Morton was sent to Palm Island as a child during the forced relocations from the Tablelands. Elsa carries these stories forward, deeply rooted in the heritage passed down through her family.

Education consultant working with PICC on the Bwgcolman Education Engagement Attainment Initiative (BEAI). Doing her PhD in engagement. Married to Mislam's cousin. Family is from Palm Island — Tennille (BEAI Coordinator) is her niece. Passionate about understanding what all PICC services provide so families can be properly referred. Interested in Indigenous data sovereignty, local servers, and physical knowledge infrastructure.

Jenni Calcraft is a passionate marine biologist and dedicated station manager at the Orpheus Island Research Station, a site owned by James Cook University. Originally from Brisbane, Jenni now thrives on Gullbody Island, where her love for the ocean meets her commitment to cultural collaboration. As a facilitator of ocean activities, she plays a vital role in supporting Native Land Title efforts and storytelling initiatives with the Munbarra people. Jenni’s unique contributions extend to fostering partnerships with Indigenous ranger groups, highlighting her dedication to both marine science and cultural respect. Her adventurous spirit and humor make each expedition memorable and impactful.

Uncle Alan Palm Island is a dedicated cultural custodian and educator, revered for his profound connection to the vibrant history and traditions of Great Palm Island. Born into the Nakawaraka skin group and fluent in Mulkaal, he deeply honors his heritage as he welcomes visitors with tales of the Mingana Mingana Ranges and the majestic manta ray that signifies his community's boundaries. His totem, the Kurenja or stingray, symbolizes his commitment to protecting the sacred customs of his people, a lesson imparted by his father alongside the art of painting. Uncle Alan's spirit of resilience and pride shapes his unique impact, as he shares the enduring knowledge and spirit of his ancestors.

Iris, a resilient cultural keeper, weaves profound narratives that celebrate her family's enduring spirit and connection to their homeland. Born to parents united in the late 1930s amid the vibrant backdrop of Maribo, Chileo, and raised amid the rich traditions of Bowan, Iris embodies the resilience of her lineage. Her stories come alive with vivid memories of her father's resourcefulness, building a boat named "Ivy May" to provide sustenance for his family. Iris captures the essence of community and survival, recounting nights spent on Possum Island hunting turtle, each tale a testament to her heritage's strength and adaptability.

Goonyun Anderson is a spirited cultural ambassador with deep ties to Palm Island, where the waters and sands resonate with his essence. Growing up in Townsville and transitioning to the vibrant life in Brisbane, Goonyun carries the heartbeat of his heritage wherever he goes. Though education took him away, his soul remains anchored in the rich traditions and landscapes of his people. Known for his authentic connection to the land and his love for fishing and hunting, Goonyun captivates with stories that spotlight the unique beauty and challenges of his beloved community.

Peggy Palm Island is a revered Mumburra elder and a cherished guardian of Palm Island's rich heritage. Born into a large family with four sisters and five brothers, she has spent nearly her entire life on Palm Island, deeply embedded in its cultural and ancestral roots. Peggy plays an influential role in the rangers program, where she imparts wisdom and confidence to younger women, sharing profound stories of her ancestors. Her vivid recounting of the lore, such as the tale of the three sisters and the creation of water on Palm Island, highlights her dedication to preserving and celebrating her community's traditions. Her passion and commitment make her an indispensable cultural beacon for future generations.

Ruby Sibley is a dedicated community advocate from Palm Island who passionately nurtures connections within her community. As an Aboriginal woman born and raised on Palm Island, Ruby cherishes the island's tranquility, often seeking solace in its natural beauty through reef walks and fishing. Her work with local schools involves engaging with parents to support and encourage consistent school attendance, demonstrating her deep commitment to the education and well-being of local children. Ruby's resilience shines through her experience of weathering storms, reflecting her strength and unwavering devotion to her home.

Richard Cassidy is a compassionate advocate dedicated to reconnecting youth with their cultural roots and educational paths. Known for his insightful conversations and deep understanding of human needs, Richard empowers disengaged young people by bridging education with heartfelt dialogue. His unique approach emphasizes identifying not just immediate needs but also deeper longings, such as enabling individuals like Max to reconnect with their homeland. Richard’s work is marked by his ability to listen intently and respond with genuine care, fostering environments where young people can thrive and find their true belonging.

The Elders Group, guided by Ben, serves as a vibrant conduit for preserving and sharing the profound stories of Australia's Indigenous communities. Rooted in the rich tapestry of places like Palm Island, Calcadun country, and beyond, Ben's journey is defined by heartfelt yarning sessions with elders like Ethel and Frank, capturing the essence of life through storms and celebrations. His collaborations with the Elders Justice Group and on projects like Orange Sky weave together narratives that reveal the nuanced, dynamic lives of these communities, aiming to create publications that reflect their resilience and wisdom. Engaged deeply in the art of storytelling, the group is dedicated to illuminating the myriad voices and experiences that shape their world, fostering understanding across Australia.

Ivy is a resilient community advocate rooted in the vibrant life of her island home. Surrounded by family and the azure expanse of the ocean, she navigates the challenges of remote living, emphasizing the importance of communal support and family connection. Striving to enhance the quality of life for her fellow islanders, Ivy highlights the struggles with accessing essential goods like beds and white goods, crafting innovative solutions such as makeshift bed frames for comfort and ease. Her dedication to uplifting her community is reflected in her commitment to fostering gathering spaces where family and friends can thrive together.

Jason Coolbra is a resilient and resourceful innovator from Palm Island, where he has lived for 49 years. Embracing both the simplicity of traditional life and the adaptability of modern living, he uniquely transforms ordinary objects into versatile, practical solutions for outdoor living. His commitment to community well-being is evident in his efforts to encourage the use of multifunctional beds that double as tables, inspired by his Aboriginal heritage. Jason's approach promotes independence and mobility, inviting others to explore the freedom of adaptable design. Trusting in his lived experience, he motivates neighboring communities to embrace these innovative ideas for a better quality of life.

profiles.bio: Benjamin Knight is a passionate storyteller and advocate for Indigenous justice, dedicated to amplifying First Nations voices within the Australian judiciary. Through his leadership in programs like the National Judicial College's Indigenous Justice Program, he brings judges onto country, fostering essential conversations that pave the way for systemic change. With deep ties to the Alice Springs community, Benjamin's work is rooted in a profound understanding of place and its significance, enhancing the dialogue between the judiciary and Indigenous communities. His commitment to creating a more equitable legal landscape makes him a respected voice in both storytelling and community advocacy.

The Elders Group, guided by Ben, serves as a vibrant conduit for preserving and sharing the profound stories of Australia's Indigenous communities. Rooted in the rich tapestry of places like Palm Island, Calcadun country, and beyond, Ben's journey is defined by heartfelt yarning sessions with elders like Ethel and Frank, capturing the essence of life through storms and celebrations. His collaborations with the Elders Justice Group and on projects like Orange Sky weave together narratives that reveal the nuanced, dynamic lives of these communities, aiming to create publications that reflect their resilience and wisdom. Engaged deeply in the art of storytelling, the group is dedicated to illuminating the myriad voices and experiences that shape their world, fostering understanding across Australia.

Aunty Ethel Taylor Robinson, a proud elder from Palm Island, carries a wealth of knowledge and experience that shapes the narrative of her community. At 78, she has been an unwavering advocate for the elders’ group, committed to preserving cultural heritage and guiding the next generation. Her roots trace back to the IRI Manga Island, where her family’s history and legacy inform her storytelling. Ethel's involvement in community meetings is marked by her dedication to speaking truthfully and respectfully, ensuring that the voices of her ancestors resonate today. Through her stories, she offers invaluable insights into the struggles and resilience of her people, making her a vital voice in cultural preservation and community empowerment.

Winifred Obah is a dynamic storyteller and community leader deeply rooted in her ancestral ties to the Mission Beach area. As the secretary for the Elders Advisory, Winifred played a pivotal role in organizing community fundraising initiatives, including baking events that fostered connection and support among local families. Her commitment to preserving cultural heritage is evident in her efforts to reconnect with the history of the Whole Har River Mission, where her family has deep roots. Winifred’s unique perspective as a descendant of the original mission families enriches her storytelling, allowing her to share powerful narratives of resilience and kinship. She embodies the spirit of community, actively engaging others in meaningful conversations about identity and belonging.

Elder Marjorie Burns is a powerful voice for resilience and cultural heritage, rooted in her experiences growing up on Palm Island. Through her storytelling, she illuminates the painful yet hopeful narratives of Aboriginal life, reflecting on the challenges of racial segregation and the loss of language within her family. Marjorie honors her ancestors, particularly her grandfather Alf Palmer, a polyglot who symbolizes the richness of Indigenous knowledge. She actively engages with her community, sharing her family’s history and advocating for the preservation of Aboriginal culture. Her stories serve as a bridge connecting past and present, inspiring others to embrace their heritage and foster a sense of belonging in their identities.

Elsa Mortoa is a passionate storyteller dedicated to preserving the rich narratives of her Aboriginal heritage. Growing up on Palm Island, she has woven her family's history into compelling tales that illuminate the struggles and triumphs of her community. With a deep understanding of cultural identity, Elsa engages audiences through storytelling initiatives that celebrate the resilience of Indigenous peoples. Her unique perspective, shaped by her mother's experiences and her own childhood, highlights the importance of oral history in bridging generations. As an advocate for cultural education, she actively participates in community workshops, ensuring that the voices of her ancestors resonate with future generations, making her a vital voice in her community.

Agnes Watton is a resilient storyteller whose life experiences reflect the strength of the human spirit. Residing on an island, she creatively navigates the challenges of home maintenance, sharing her journey through evocative narratives that resonate with her community. Agnes’s artistic flair shines through in her photography, which captures the essence of her surroundings, including a collaborative project featured in a local building. Her voice serves as a beacon of hope and inspiration, illustrating the importance of perseverance amidst adversity. As a dedicated community member, Agnes's stories not only entertain but also foster connections, making her an invaluable voice in local dialogues about resilience and creativity.

Men's Group is a transformative collective born from the shared experiences of overcoming addiction and seeking genuine purpose beyond substances. Originating from personal struggles with alcohol, they inspire others to define their own daily motivations and break free from dependency. Unlike the mindless routines dictated by addiction, they emphasize intentional living and self-determined goals. Through candid dialogue, Men's Group empowers individuals to reevaluate their priorities, fostering a profound sense of satisfaction and accomplishment. Their unique contribution lies in guiding others to craft authentic purposes and enrich their everyday lives with intentionality and resilience.

Jacinta Gaia is a passionate community leader and storyteller with a rich background in coaching and event coordination at the Palm Island Community Hub. Her dedication to fostering connections between youth and elders shines through innovative initiatives like the pilot mowing program, which not only assists elders in aged care but also provides young participants with valuable life skills and mentorship. Jacinta's vision extends to organizing food hampers and coordinating peak community events, ensuring every voice is heard and represented. Through her work, she inspires a spirit of collaboration and resilience, empowering the next generation to take active roles in their community.

The PICC Women's Healing Service Team is dedicated to empowering First Nations women at risk or involved in the criminal justice system across Townsville and Palm Island. With a holistic approach, they tailor support to each woman's unique journey, addressing issues like domestic violence, mental health, and substance abuse. Key initiatives include the Women's Yarning Circle, co-designed with Elders, and the TARS Health Clinic, which addresses critical healthcare gaps post-custody. The team actively collaborates with local charities to provide essential resources, ensuring dignity and hope for every client. Averaging 86-130 clients per quarter, they stand as a vital community voice, advocating for systemic change and fostering resilience among women in need.

Uncle George Leon, a distinguished Kalkadoon elder, embodies resilience and community spirit in Mount Isa, where his roots run deep since his family's arrival in 1947. Raised in a vibrant household that fostered camaraderie among thousands, including local rugby league players, Uncle George learned the value of community early on. Known for his 'tough love' approach, he has dedicated his life to steering youth towards education, emphasizing the importance of engaging with children in school to curb crime and nurture potential. His unique role as a PLO was instrumental in connecting with young people and reinforcing community trust, making Uncle George a beloved and respected figure whose impact spans generations.
PICC Women's Shelter Team shares valuable insights and experiences through their storytelling.

His journey as a refugee has been both challenging and rewarding, offering profound lessons amidst the adversities of homelessness. Despite losing trust in institutions, Freddy's determination shines as he strives for dignity and essential rights, underscoring the universal need for shelter and community. His experiences in both Fiji and Australia highlight the complexities of adapting to new environments, where punctuality and survival intertwine in unexpected ways. Freddy's story is a testament to perseverance, showcasing his unwavering commitment to rebuilding his life.

Born in Townsville and raised on the resilient lands of Palm Island, Natalie carries the legacy of her ancestors who were forcibly relocated under the Aboriginal Protection Act. Her life's journey, steeped in the traditions of Cape York Peninsula and enriched by her role as a Bulgaman woman, fuels her commitment to cultural preservation and natural resource management. Through her impactful work with the Working on Country Ranger Program and her efforts with NQ Dry Tropics and the North Queensland Land Council, Natalie intricately weaves ancestral wisdom into modern environmental stewardship, empowering her community and honoring her storied lineage.

Tammy is a dedicated storyteller and consultant with a profound commitment to Indigenous education and data sovereignty. As part of the Bwgcolman Education Engagement Attainment Initiative (BEAI), she champions holistic support from early childhood through secondary education, enhancing community engagement and attendance. Her work emphasizes the importance of a comprehensive service directory to connect families with essential resources. Tammy's passion for preserving cultural heritage is evident in her advocacy for local knowledge infrastructure and historical site preservation, including the Fantome Island initiative. With a PhD focus on engagement, she uniquely weaves community narratives into her projects, serving as a vital voice for Indigenous perspectives.

Patricia Doyle is a passionate Aboriginal storyteller from the 40 tribes of Erman, deeply rooted in her community of Palm. With a unique perspective shaped by her experiences during the recent storm, Patricia emphasizes the importance of resilience and togetherness in times of crisis. As a dedicated community member, she actively engages in both customer support at a local tile shop and local initiatives that foster community connections. Patricia believes in the power of storytelling to capture the lived experiences of her people and to highlight the strength of community bonds. Her insights into preparedness and response during natural disasters make her a vital voice in advocating for better support systems and resources for her community.
Co-presented "Proud Bwgcolman Youth" at the 2023 SNAICC Conference, sharing a hopeful narrative about Palm Island young people.

Ruby Sibley is a dedicated community advocate from Palm Island who passionately nurtures connections within her community. As an Aboriginal woman born and raised on Palm Island, Ruby cherishes the island's tranquility, often seeking solace in its natural beauty through reef walks and fishing. Her work with local schools involves engaging with parents to support and encourage consistent school attendance, demonstrating her deep commitment to the education and well-being of local children. Ruby's resilience shines through her experience of weathering storms, reflecting her strength and unwavering devotion to her home.

Jacqui is a dedicated public health advocate and compassionate volunteer with Orange Sky, a group committed to enhancing dignity and fostering genuine connections among those in need. Originally joining the cause in 2018, Jacqui brings a wealth of experience from her public health background. Her passion for service is deeply personal, driven by her familiarity with diverse social backgrounds and the impact of witnessing a family member facing difficult times. Known for her empathetic nature, Jacqui finds joy in continuing meaningful conversations with the friends she’s made, reinforcing the community’s spirit through simple, yet profound acts of kindness like providing laundry services.

Jeanie Sam is a passionate storyteller and advocate for the empowerment of children and families on Palm Island. As a leader in the Bwgcolman Way initiative, Jeanie champions community-led care arrangements for vulnerable children, ensuring that all Manbarra and Bwgcolman children are nurtured within their cultural roots. Drawing strength from the resilience of their ancestors, Jeanie emphasizes the importance of community connections and Elders' guidance in shaping service delivery that is deeply rooted in place. With decades of advocacy behind them, Jeanie's voice resonates powerfully, inspiring change and fostering a brighter future for the next generation. Their commitment to community control and cultural integrity makes them an invaluable asset to the Palm Island community.

Ida Richardson is a passionate storyteller and community advocate, deeply rooted in the traditions of her culture. As a full-time mother and grandmother, she shares her love for traditional dance, a lifelong practice that connects her to her heritage and enriches her community. Ida emphasizes the importance of community support in parenting, expressing her desire for more activities to engage children on Palm Island. Her commitment to fostering a nurturing environment is evident as she collaborates on projects that inspire creativity among the youth, such as the upcoming initiative involving local photography. Through her storytelling, Ida brings invaluable insights that highlight the strength of communal ties and the joy of cultural expression.

Hayley Wetzel is a dedicated early childhood education manager at the Child and Family Center, where she plays a pivotal role in supporting families and children in the community. With a focus on inclusivity and resilience, Hayley has successfully navigated the challenges posed by recent flooding, leading initiatives to reopen the center and implement feedback-driven improvements, such as a sensory room tailored for various age groups. Her expertise in parental support and community engagement shines through various workshops and home visits, fostering a nurturing environment for families. Hayley envisions a thriving future for the center, aiming to enhance community connections and resources over the next two decades.

Nayden is a vibrant young storyteller from Mount Isa, whose candid insights reflect the realities of youth in his community. With a keen understanding of local life, Nayden sheds light on the challenges faced by young people, including boredom and mischief, while also expressing a desire for connection with nature. His experiences at Spinifex School, where he enjoys spending time with friends and engaging in activities like gym workouts, showcase his commitment to personal growth and community involvement. Nayden's unique perspective as a youth in Mount Isa makes him a valuable voice for his peers, advocating for positive engagement and outdoor exploration, and inspiring others to connect with their surroundings and each other.

Jess Smit is an inspiring educator and project officer deeply rooted in Townsville, where she has spent the last 18 years transforming young lives. With a strong foundation in teaching, Jess has passionately devoted herself to the ATSIMS program, working with First Nations high school students to explore marine science and foster new experiences on the reef. Her journey comes full circle on Palm Island, where she began her career, now collaborating with the Orpheus Island Station Crew, the Minga Minga Ranger Group, and the Mumba Traditional Owners. Jess is driven by a profound sense of purpose in empowering others, especially women in marine environments, celebrating shared stories and traditions while building capacities within her community.

Peggy Palm Island is a revered Mumburra elder and a cherished guardian of Palm Island's rich heritage. Born into a large family with four sisters and five brothers, she has spent nearly her entire life on Palm Island, deeply embedded in its cultural and ancestral roots. Peggy plays an influential role in the rangers program, where she imparts wisdom and confidence to younger women, sharing profound stories of her ancestors. Her vivid recounting of the lore, such as the tale of the three sisters and the creation of water on Palm Island, highlights her dedication to preserving and celebrating her community's traditions. Her passion and commitment make her an indispensable cultural beacon for future generations.

Uncle Alan Palm Island is a dedicated cultural custodian and educator, revered for his profound connection to the vibrant history and traditions of Great Palm Island. Born into the Nakawaraka skin group and fluent in Mulkaal, he deeply honors his heritage as he welcomes visitors with tales of the Mingana Mingana Ranges and the majestic manta ray that signifies his community's boundaries. His totem, the Kurenja or stingray, symbolizes his commitment to protecting the sacred customs of his people, a lesson imparted by his father alongside the art of painting. Uncle Alan's spirit of resilience and pride shapes his unique impact, as he shares the enduring knowledge and spirit of his ancestors.

Henry Doyle is a dedicated youth advocate and proud Aboriginal resident of Palm Island, whose passion for his community is evident in his work with disengaged youth. Living on the island since birth, Henry combines his love for sports, particularly footy, with his commitment to keeping young people active and engaged. Through his role in youth services, he designs programs that offer youth meaningful opportunities, from securing driver's and boat licenses to participating in community service. His efforts extend to nearby Townsville, where he supports the next generation by cultivating a sense of belonging and resilience, ensuring they steer clear of mischief. Henry's vibrant presence and unwavering dedication make a profound impact, offering hope and guidance to those who need it most.

Alice, an unwavering community pillar, navigates the intricate world of childcare with resilience and compassion. A cornerstone during challenging times, she transforms setbacks into opportunities for growth, ensuring continuity and care for her community. Her unwavering dedication, even as critical infrastructure faced disruptions, showcases her commitment to maintaining stability and nurturing an environment where everyone feels supported. Whether mopping floors or orchestrating routines amidst chaos, Alice’s leadership shines, reflecting her steadfast commitment to the well-being of the children and families she serves.

Playgroup Staff is a dedicated team of daycare workers who have shown resilience and compassion in the face of adversity. Following a devastating flood that impacted their center, they have played a crucial role in supporting the early childhood community by adapting to challenging circumstances. Their firsthand experiences during the crisis highlight the emotional weight and logistical hurdles faced by caregivers and families alike. With a focus on maintaining continuity for children, they have worked diligently to keep services running, even in temporary conditions. Their commitment to fostering a nurturing environment is evident as they navigate recovery efforts, ensuring that play and learning remain accessible for all.

A dedicated cultural storyteller, Rangers and Elders Conversation reflects the wisdom of his elders while bridging generations through the rich tapestry of Aboriginal heritage. With a deep understanding of his community's roots, he passionately shares stories and teachings that span over 70,000 years of culture. His commitment to preserving and revitalizing traditions is evident in his efforts to educate younger generations about their language, tribal names, and cultural identities. By fostering connections among community members and encouraging youth participation, he plays a pivotal role in keeping his culture alive. His heartfelt narratives not only honor the past but inspire the future, making him an invaluable voice in the community.

Rachel Atkinson, CEO of Palm Island Community Company, is a passionate advocate for community-led initiatives and social welfare. With over 17 years of dedicated service, Rachel has transformed PICC into a model of community control that employs over 217 staff, 87% of whom are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Under her leadership, the organization has developed vital services, including a safe house for children, a medical center, and holistic early childhood programs. Rachel believes in the power of legacy, striving to ensure every child feels safe and proud of their culture. Her commitment to fostering a healthy community culture inspires ownership and pride among staff and families, making her a pivotal voice for empowerment and change.

Elder Ude Grace Richardson is a cherished storyteller and a pillar of the Palm Island community. With a rich tapestry of experiences, she draws from her life marked by resilience, familial challenges, and deep connections. Ude's journey from Georgetown to Palm Island has been shaped by her commitment to fostering a sense of belonging among her peers. Her recent involvement with the elders' group has allowed her to share invaluable lessons of humility and respect, enriching the lives of those around her. Ude’s storytelling emphasizes the power of community and the wisdom gained from shared experiences, making her a vital voice in preserving cultural narratives and fostering intergenerational dialogue.

Roy Prior is a devoted community advocate and cultural custodian, deeply rooted in the serene landscapes of Palmer. Returning to his island home to care for his ailing grandmother, Roy has since become an integral part of the community's fabric, cherishing its natural beauty and laid-back spirit. His extensive family ties stretch across the region, from Townsville to Cairns, fostering connections that reflect his commitment to kinship and community. Since joining the local initiative, Pick, in 2011, Roy has been pivotal in grassroots efforts, enhancing community engagement and support. His enduring presence and dedication have left a lasting impact, strengthening bonds and nurturing the island's vibrant cultural tapestry.

Aunty Iris May Whitey is a revered storyteller and cultural custodian, passionately dedicated to preserving and sharing the rich narratives of her heritage. Hailing from a large family and the stolen generation, Aunty Iris draws from her personal history to highlight the resilience of her community, emphasizing the importance of cultural identity and language. She actively engages in storytelling, ensuring that the wisdom of her ancestors is passed down to future generations. Through community initiatives, Aunty Iris teaches the significance of knowing one's roots and maintaining connections to family and culture. Her unwavering commitment to education and cultural preservation makes her a vital voice in her community, inspiring others to honor their histories and identities.

Clay Alfred is a passionate storyteller and community advocate, dedicated to empowering marginalized voices through narrative. With a rich background in community engagement, he emphasizes the importance of connection over formalities, believing that conversations drive genuine support. Clay has been instrumental in initiatives aimed at revitalizing youth programs, including the establishment of bike sheds for local youths and reviving scouting activities for young people. His work focuses on ensuring that storytellers maintain ownership of their narratives, enabling them to leverage their stories for community benefit. Clay's insightful approach fosters empowerment and understanding, making him a vital voice in community development.

Irene Nleallajar is a passionate cultural custodian from Palm Island, deeply rooted in the rich heritage of the Boman people. Born in Townsville in the 1960s, Irene has witnessed remarkable changes across decades, drawing strength from her family's legacy of resilience and connection to the land. Her father's legacy as a fisherman and bush walker, as well as being the eldest of fifteen, informs her profound connection to nature and community. Irene embodies the spirit of her ancestors, sharing their stories and traditions with the next generation while celebrating the vibrant history and beauty of Palm Island.

Jenni Calcraft is a passionate marine biologist and dedicated station manager at the Orpheus Island Research Station, a site owned by James Cook University. Originally from Brisbane, Jenni now thrives on Gullbody Island, where her love for the ocean meets her commitment to cultural collaboration. As a facilitator of ocean activities, she plays a vital role in supporting Native Land Title efforts and storytelling initiatives with the Munbarra people. Jenni’s unique contributions extend to fostering partnerships with Indigenous ranger groups, highlighting her dedication to both marine science and cultural respect. Her adventurous spirit and humor make each expedition memorable and impactful.

Iris is a dedicated caseworker devoted to empowering women navigating the challenges of substance abuse and everyday struggles. With a compassionate, supportive approach, Iris fosters resilience and community bonds, making a profound impact in her role. Part of the Ferdys team, she connects deeply with those she helps, becoming a trusted figure within the community. Her work extends beyond individual support to nurturing a welcoming environment where women can share stories and craft connections, reinforcing a sense of family and belonging.
Chairperson of the PICC Board, pivotal in the 2021 transition to full community control.
PICC Board Director since founding (2007). 40+ years across community, public, and academic sectors.
PICC Board Director. FCPA, Graduate AICD. Financial governance expertise.
PICC Community Director. Born and raised on Palm Island. Experience from Townsville Aboriginal and Islander Legal Service.
PICC Board Director. 14 years legal expertise in Native Title and Indigenous Cultural Heritage. Founder of Parallax Legal.

Goonyun Anderson is a spirited cultural ambassador with deep ties to Palm Island, where the waters and sands resonate with his essence. Growing up in Townsville and transitioning to the vibrant life in Brisbane, Goonyun carries the heartbeat of his heritage wherever he goes. Though education took him away, his soul remains anchored in the rich traditions and landscapes of his people. Known for his authentic connection to the land and his love for fishing and hunting, Goonyun captivates with stories that spotlight the unique beauty and challenges of his beloved community.

Iris, a resilient cultural keeper, weaves profound narratives that celebrate her family's enduring spirit and connection to their homeland. Born to parents united in the late 1930s amid the vibrant backdrop of Maribo, Chileo, and raised amid the rich traditions of Bowan, Iris embodies the resilience of her lineage. Her stories come alive with vivid memories of her father's resourcefulness, building a boat named "Ivy May" to provide sustenance for his family. Iris captures the essence of community and survival, recounting nights spent on Possum Island hunting turtle, each tale a testament to her heritage's strength and adaptability.
Bwgcolman man. Lifelong Palm Island resident. Teachers Aide at Bwgcolman Community School. Appointed to PICC Board 2023.

Allan Palm Island is a passionate storyteller and artist dedicated to sharing the rich cultural heritage of Palm Island. Trained by his father, Allan's artistic journey reflects a deep connection to family and community, emphasizing the importance of togetherness in his work. He believes in the power of education to bridge cultural gaps and foster understanding. Through various initiatives, Allan inspires young artists, encouraging them to explore their creativity and engage with traditional techniques. His contributions include workshops that teach indigenous art forms, promoting cultural pride and storytelling through visual arts. Allan's unique perspective as an Indigenous artist makes him a vital voice in the community, championing the stories and traditions of his people.

Cyndel Louise Pryor is a passionate storyteller and cultural advocate from Palm Island, dedicated to preserving and sharing the rich histories of her ancestors. As a member of the Stolen Generations, Cyndel's work is deeply rooted in her family's experiences and the legacy of her mother, who navigated the challenging terrain of identity and belonging. Cyndel actively seeks to reconnect with her mother's heritage, engaging with communities to uncover lost narratives. Her contributions include leading initiatives that uplift Indigenous voices and promote cultural understanding. Through her storytelling, Cyndel inspires others to honor their roots and foster connections that bridge generations, making her a vital voice in her community.

Uncle Frank Daniel Landers: A resilient and reflective soul, Uncle Frank Daniel Landers hails from Ingham and carries the weight of history with an unyielding spirit. Born in 1945, his journey began when his family relocated and faced the trials of dormitory life, shaping his formative years with discipline and community reliance. Despite the challenges of being governed by strict rules, Uncle Frank found strength in his roots and eventually embraced life beyond the confines of the boys' homes. His unique narrative, spanning from separation during his mother's illness to mingling with newfound freedom in the village, is a testament to his endurance and adaptability. Through his vivid recollections, Uncle Frank shares a profound legacy of perseverance and connection to heritage.

Jason Coolbra is a resilient and resourceful innovator from Palm Island, where he has lived for 49 years. Embracing both the simplicity of traditional life and the adaptability of modern living, he uniquely transforms ordinary objects into versatile, practical solutions for outdoor living. His commitment to community well-being is evident in his efforts to encourage the use of multifunctional beds that double as tables, inspired by his Aboriginal heritage. Jason's approach promotes independence and mobility, inviting others to explore the freedom of adaptable design. Trusting in his lived experience, he motivates neighboring communities to embrace these innovative ideas for a better quality of life.

Ethel and Iris Ferdies are pillars of resilience and familial devotion, deeply rooted in their dynamic family history. Growing up in a family of 17, Ethel and Iris witnessed firsthand the struggles and triumphs of their mother, a member of the Stolen Generations, who raised them with fortitude and love. Despite the hardships of their upbringing—defined by chores, loss, and the need to support each other—Ethel speaks warmly of the lessons learned from their mother, a woman of the Durable tribe with exceptional culinary skills. Both sisters, marked by the legacy of displacement yet anchored by community and tradition, embody strength and unity through their lived experiences. Their narratives are a testament to overcoming adversity and maintaining cultural heritage.

Ivy is a resilient community advocate rooted in the vibrant life of her island home. Surrounded by family and the azure expanse of the ocean, she navigates the challenges of remote living, emphasizing the importance of communal support and family connection. Striving to enhance the quality of life for her fellow islanders, Ivy highlights the struggles with accessing essential goods like beds and white goods, crafting innovative solutions such as makeshift bed frames for comfort and ease. Her dedication to uplifting her community is reflected in her commitment to fostering gathering spaces where family and friends can thrive together.

Luigi is a vibrant youth advocate and storyteller from Mount Isa, bringing a fresh perspective on youth engagement and health. With a passion for fitness and well-being, he emphasizes the importance of physical strength in fostering self-esteem among young people. Luigi actively participates in community initiatives, such as the Youth Hub and Headspace, where he engages with peers to promote positive lifestyles. His experiences at Spinifex school, where he excels in math and English, showcase his commitment to education. Through his storytelling, Luigi inspires fellow youth to embrace their strengths and contribute to their community, making him a valuable voice in shaping the future of Mount Isa.

Marilyn Laner, a devoted and vibrant pillar of the Kora community for over 40 years, embodies connection and compassion every Wednesday morning. A true "people collector," Marilyn's rich experiences span from her early days in the Boyan Valley to her extensive volunteer work with children and the elderly. Despite personal hardships, including the loss of two brothers and overcoming societal challenges alongside her Italian husband, Marilyn has remained steadfast in her belief in the power of human connection. Her warmth and advocacy for the less fortunate, whether through lending an ear or assembling warm meals, illuminate her enduring legacy of giving. Marilyn's life is a testament to the strength found in shared stories and the vibrant community she continues to nurture.

Paige Tanner Hill is a passionate marine advocate from Palm Island, dedicated to preserving and connecting with their rich cultural heritage. At just 18, Paige is making waves as a junior ranger, immersing themselves in diving ventures and historical explorations to understand the past and rejuvenate community ties. A recent graduate of Wuggerman Community School, they are committed to engaging local youth in environmental stewardship through the ranger program, blending modern initiatives with ancestral wisdom. With each dive at sites like the Two Sisters reef, Paige uncovers a breathtaking underwater world, eager to share its stories and their unyielding beauty.

Richard Cassidy is a compassionate advocate dedicated to reconnecting youth with their cultural roots and educational paths. Known for his insightful conversations and deep understanding of human needs, Richard empowers disengaged young people by bridging education with heartfelt dialogue. His unique approach emphasizes identifying not just immediate needs but also deeper longings, such as enabling individuals like Max to reconnect with their homeland. Richard’s work is marked by his ability to listen intently and respond with genuine care, fostering environments where young people can thrive and find their true belonging.

Context: Describing her emotional experience visiting her mother's country during the elders' trip

stories.title: I think the journey will never end. That's why it's so im...
stories.content:

Context: Discussing whether there's an end to the healing journey and her commitment to sharing family history

stories.title: I was excited inside. Spiritually, it just lifted me up t...

stories.title: I think it's my job to do that closure for mom. I'm not s...
